1: Deuteronomy 5 opens the second major speech of Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 5:1–Deuteronomy 28:68), the law-code proper. Moses summons all Israel to hear the statutes and ordinances that constitute the covenant law. The phrase "all Israel" (kol Yisrael) is characteristic of Deuteronomy's rhetorical strategy: this is a plenary assembly, not a gathering of representatives or elders only. The covenant is addressed to the whole people, which distinguishes it from ANE vassal-treaty practice where only the king and his court received the document. In Deuteronomy, every Israelite is a treaty-party with YHWH.
2: "The LORD our God made a covenant with us at Horeb" — the historical grounding: the law that follows is covenant-renewal, not new legislation. The covenant at Horeb/Sinai is the foundational legal event. Deuteronomy regularly refers to Sinai as "Horeb" — the name may reflect a northern tradition (used in Elijah's story, 1 Kings 19:8) while Exodus/Numbers use "Sinai" (possibly a southern tradition). Both names refer to the same mountain; the variant usage is a linguistic marker of Deuteronomy's distinct literary tradition within the Pentateuch.
3: "Not with our fathers did the LORD make this covenant, but with us, who are all of us here alive today" — the contemporizing principle: the covenant is addressed to the present generation as direct parties, not as inheritors of a past agreement. This is a deliberate rhetorical move: Moses is speaking to the children of the Exodus generation, yet declares the Horeb covenant was made "with us here today." The covenant is not historical in the sense of past and over — it is perpetually contemporaneous. Each generation stands before YHWH as if present at Sinai. This principle shapes all subsequent Jewish interpretation of Exodus and Deuteronomy: the Passover Haggadah institutionalizes it ("In every generation, each person is obligated to see himself as if he personally left Egypt").
4: "The LORD spoke with you face to face at the mountain, out of the midst of the fire" — the Horeb theophany recalled: YHWH spoke directly, not through an intermediary, to the whole assembly. This direct divine speech to an entire nation is presented as unique and unprecedented (Deuteronomy 4:33: "Did any people ever hear the voice of a god speaking out of the midst of fire, as you have heard, and still live?"). The claim of national-direct revelation is one of Deuteronomy's most distinctive theological arguments: the covenant rests on a publicly witnessed event, not private tradition.
5: Moses as mediator: "while I stood between the LORD and you at that time, to declare to you the word of the LORD, for you were afraid because of the fire" — the mediatorial role of Moses is here explained as arising from the people's fear of the divine fire. In Exodus 20:18-20 the people request Moses's mediation after hearing YHWH's voice. Deuteronomy presents this as the historical context for Moses's unique role. The mediator-pattern — YHWH speaks to Moses, Moses speaks to the people — is the structural model for all subsequent prophetic revelation in Israel.
6: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery" — the prologue to the Decalogue: the same self-identification formula appears in both recensions (Exodus 20:2 and Deuteronomy 5:6). In Hittite suzerainty treaty form, this corresponds to the preamble section (identifying the great king) and the historical prologue (recounting the king's benefits to the vassal). YHWH identifies himself not as creator-God abstractly but as the specific God who performed the Exodus — the covenant is grounded in historical act, not metaphysical claim.
7: "You shall have no other gods before me" — the first commandment in its ANE context: the surrounding Canaanite world practiced monolatry (worship of one primary deity within a pantheon) rather than monotheism. The Ugaritic texts (discovered at Ras Shamra beginning 1929) show a pantheon dominated by El and Baal with dozens of associated deities. The Decalogue's first commandment does not deny the existence of other divine beings but prohibits their worship: "before my face" (al panai) means YHWH's presence tolerates no rival worship. The practical monotheism of Deuteronomy becomes explicit theoretical monotheism in Deutero-Isaiah (Isaiah 44:6: "besides me there is no God").
8: The prohibition of images (pesel, H6459): the second commandment prohibits carved images of anything in heaven, earth, or water — not just images of other gods but images of YHWH himself. The Egyptian religious world was saturated with divine images; the Canaanite cult used standing stones (masseboth) and Asherah poles. Israel's imageless worship was so distinctive that Greek and Roman observers commented on it (Josephus, Tacitus, Juvenal all note the absence of divine images in the Jerusalem temple). The aniconism of Israelite worship is one of its most archaeologically confirmed characteristics — the absence of divine figurines in Israelite contexts (though not universal) contrasts with the ubiquity of such items in Canaanite sites.
9: "For I the LORD your God am a jealous God (el qanna), visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me" — the jealousy (qanna, H7067) of YHWH reflects the covenant's exclusive-loyalty structure. Ancient treaties imposed exclusive loyalty obligations: the vassal could not have treaty relations with the suzerain's enemies. The generational consequence language reflects solidarity thinking: families share in consequences of the head's covenant loyalty or disloyalty. The Babylonian Talmud (Sanhedrin 27b-28a) wrestled with this in relation to Deuteronomy 24:16's individual-responsibility principle, concluding the generational consequence refers to those who continue in the parents' sin.
10: "But showing steadfast love (chesed, H2617) to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments" — the asymmetry is deliberate and theologically significant: sin visits to three-four generations; chesed extends to thousands (of generations). The ratio is approximately Deuteronomy 2500:4. The covenant's mercy far outweighs its judgment in scope and duration. Chesed (H2617) is the covenant-loyalty term: YHWH's loyal love to those who keep the covenant. This is the chesed that Ruth shows Naomi (Ruth 1:8; Deuteronomy 3:10) and that defines YHWH's character (Exodus 34:6-7: "abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness").
11: The third commandment: "You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain" — the Hebrew lo tissa et-shem YHWH eloheicha lashav (do not lift up the name of YHWH your God to emptiness/falsehood). The commandment primarily addresses false oaths — invoking YHWH's name as guarantor of a false statement. In the ancient world, oaths sworn in a deity's name carried enormous legal and religious weight; using that name for false oaths or frivolous purposes was a direct assault on YHWH's honor and the community's trust infrastructure. The secondary application to careless speech is also included in the command's scope.
12: The Sabbath commandment in Deuteronomy 5: "Observe the Sabbath day, to keep it holy, as the LORD your God commanded you" — the Deuteronomy version uses shamor (observe/keep) while Exodus 20:8 uses zachor (remember). The rabbinic tradition harmonized these by noting both were spoken simultaneously at Sinai (zachor veshamor bedibur echad). The substantive difference is the motivational ground: Exodus 20 grounds the Sabbath in creation (God rested on the seventh day); Deuteronomy 5 grounds it in the Exodus ("Remember that you were a slave in Egypt," v15). The two grounds are complementary: creation-order (cosmic) and redemption-history (covenant).
13: "Six days you shall labor and do all your work" — the six-day workweek as the Sabbath's context: the Sabbath is meaningful because it interrupts regular labor. The six-day work cycle may be contrasted with Babylonian approaches to time — the Babylonian shappatu was an unlucky day associated with the full moon, not a weekly-recurring day of rest. The seven-day week with a recurring day of abstention from work is a distinctive Israelite institution that the ancient world eventually adopted through Jewish and Christian influence. Canaanite and Egyptian calendars did not have a seven-day week as a regular unit.
14: "But the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God. On it you shall not do any work, you or your son or your daughter or your male servant or your female servant, or your ox or your donkey or any of your livestock, or the sojourner who is within your gates, that your male servant and your female servant may rest as well as you" — the inclusion of servants and sojourners: the Sabbath is the most radically equalizing institution of the ancient world. Slaves, who had no legal personhood in most ANE contexts, receive one day per week of the same rest as their masters. The Deuteronomy version adds "your ox and your donkey and any livestock" — extending the rest to working animals. This ecological dimension of Sabbath is distinctive.
15: "You shall remember that you were a slave in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God brought you out from there with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm" — the Exodus-memory grounds the Deuteronomic Sabbath. The person who was a slave knows what it means not to rest; the liberated person cannot impose on another what they themselves suffered. The Exodus functions throughout Deuteronomy as the ethical conscience: remember your vulnerability, and protect the vulnerable now within your care. This memory-ethic is Deuteronomy's distinctive contribution to moral theology.
16: "Honor your father and your mother, as the LORD your God commanded you, that your days may be long, and that it may go well with you in the land that the LORD your God is giving you" — the fifth commandment with the Deuteronomic additions: Exodus 20:12 has "that your days may be long in the land"; Deuteronomy 5:16 adds "as the LORD commanded you" (linking it to the covenant's specificity) and "that it may go well with you" (the Deuteronomic blessing formula). Parents are the primary conduit of covenant tradition (Deuteronomy 6:20-25): honoring parents is not merely filial piety but covenant faithfulness — the parents embody the covenant memory that the children must receive.
17: "You shall not murder" — the sixth commandment: lo tirtsach (H7523, murder/kill with intent). The Hebrew verb distinguishes intentional killing (ratsach) from accidental killing (harag or hemit) — only the former is prohibited by this commandment. The cities of refuge (Deut 19) are designed for the accidental killer who needs protection from blood vengeance. Capital punishment and warfare are authorized under other commandments: the sixth commandment establishes the sanctity of human life specifically against personal violence, not all taking of life. ANE law codes (Code of Hammurabi, Middle Assyrian Laws) also prohibited murder but linked it to compensation and social status — Deuteronomy does not permit monetary substitution for murder.
18: "And you shall not commit adultery" — the seventh commandment: lo tinaaf (H5003, commit adultery). Ancient Near Eastern law codes generally prohibited adultery but defined it differently: the Code of Hammurabi §129 defined adultery as a married woman's intercourse with another man (not a married man's intercourse with an unmarried woman). The Deuteronomic commandment is parallel: it addresses the married woman's exclusive sexual covenant with her husband. The covenant-structure of marriage (the woman's sexual loyalty as covenant-symbol) is the theological framework, not merely a property-rights concern.
19: "And you shall not steal" — the eighth commandment: lo tignov (H1589). Like the sixth commandment, the specific Hebrew verb matters: ganav primarily meant to steal by stealth or deception (as opposed to robbery by force, gazal, H1497). Talmudic tradition (Sanhedrin 86a) identified this as primarily a prohibition of man-stealing (kidnapping): the earlier commands prohibit murder and adultery (capital offenses against persons); this one prohibits kidnapping (the capital offense of Deuteronomy 24:7). The general property-theft application was understood as included by extension.
20: "And you shall not bear false witness against your neighbor" — the ninth commandment: lo ta'aneh vera'cha ed shav (do not answer your neighbor as a false witness). The legal context is explicit: this is court testimony, not general lying. False testimony in the gate/court system was a capital offense under the lex talionis of Deuteronomy 19:16-21. The commandment protects the judicial system's integrity — without reliable testimony, the entire covenant-justice structure collapses. The Proverbs tradition makes false witness one of the seven things YHWH hates (Proverbs 6:19).
21: "And you shall not covet your neighbor's wife. And you shall not desire your neighbor's house, his field, or his male servant, or his female servant, his ox, or his donkey, or anything that is your neighbor's" — the tenth commandment in Deuteronomy: the order reverses Exodus 20:17 (which lists "house" first, then "wife"). This may reflect different social settings: Exodus 20 addresses a more nomadic context (house = tent), while Deuteronomy 5 reflects settled-agricultural life (field added explicitly). The two verbs differ: lo tachmod (Exod 20) vs. lo tachmod... velo titavveh (Deut 5) — the second verse adds hitavveh (desire/crave), addressing the internal appetite that produces the external action. This is the only commandment addressing inner desire; Augustine and Luther both noted its significance for understanding the law's total claim on the inner life.
22: "These words the LORD spoke to all your assembly at the mountain out of the midst of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness, with a loud voice; and he added no more" — the sealing formula: YHWH spoke these Ten Words directly to the assembly and then stopped. The Decalogue is the only portion of Torah delivered directly to the whole assembly without Mosaic mediation. "He added no more" (velo yasaf) may indicate the Decalogue is complete in itself, a closed utterance. The tablets of stone then contain these words written by God's own finger (v22b: "he wrote them on two tablets of stone and gave them to me").
23: The people's response: "And as soon as you heard the voice out of the midst of the darkness, while the mountain was burning with fire, you came near to me, all the heads of your tribes, and your elders" — the tribal-elder representative structure: in the moment of crisis, the formal leadership structure is invoked. The rashei shivteichem (heads of your tribes) and zeqenichem (elders) come forward as the community's official representatives to communicate the people's request for Mosaic mediation. This delegation moment formalizes Moses's mediatorial role.
24: "Behold, the LORD our God has shown us his glory and greatness, and we have heard his voice out of the midst of the fire. This day we have seen God speak with man, and man still live" — the theological observation: divine-human dialogue without death was considered impossible by the ancient world generally. Egyptian texts describe the terrifying overwhelming nature of divine presence; the OT itself repeatedly notes that seeing YHWH directly brings death (Exodus 33:20; Judges 13:22; Isaiah 6:5). The fact that Israel survived hearing YHWH's voice at Sinai is recorded as exceptional and evidence of YHWH's gracious intention toward them.
25: "Now therefore why should we die? For this great fire will consume us. If we hear the voice of the LORD our God any more, we shall die" — the people's theological analysis of their vulnerability: continued direct divine speech would be lethal. The argument is not fear-driven cowardice but accurate theological assessment: sustained exposure to the full weight of YHWH's presence exceeds what embodied humans can sustain. The request for Mosaic mediation is theologically sound — it is the covenant community's recognition of its creaturely limitation before the Creator's holiness.
26: "For who is there of all flesh that has heard the voice of the living God speaking out of the midst of fire as we have, and has still lived?" — the rhetorical question implies an unprecedented event. The phrase "living God" (elohim chayyim) distinguishes YHWH from the inert idols of the nations (Jeremiah 10:10; 1 Thessalonians 1:9): this is the God who is alive, active, speaking — and whose speech is therefore of a different order from human speech. The expression becomes a standard NT formula for YHWH's identity (Matthew 16:16; Acts 14:15; Hebrews 10:31).
27: "Go near and hear all that the LORD our God will say, and speak to us all that the LORD our God will speak to you, and we will hear and do it" — the covenantal acceptance of the mediatorial structure: the people commit to hearing YHWH's word through Moses and obeying it. The double formula nishma veasinu (we will hear and do) is the covenantal response formula, matching the Exodus 24:7 formula at the original covenant ceremony. The people's consent to the mediatorial arrangement is itself a covenantal act.
28: "And the LORD heard your words, when you spoke to me. And the LORD said to me, 'I have heard the words of this people, which they have spoken to you. They are right in all that they have spoken'" — YHWH approves the people's assessment of their own situation and their request for mediation. The divine approval of the request prevents any implication that Mosaic mediation represents a failure of faith or access. YHWH himself validates the arrangement: the covenant people's recognition of their need for mediation is not timidity but theological accuracy.
29: "Oh that they had such a heart as this always, to fear me and to keep all my commandments, that it might go well with them and with their descendants forever!" — the divine wish: YHWH expresses desire for the continued faithfulness of the heart-posture the people currently display at Horeb. The condition at Horeb — fear, willingness to hear, commitment to do — is the ideal covenant relationship. The "Oh that..." (mi yitten) is the wish-particle expressing that this ideal will not in fact be maintained. Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26-27 are the prophetic answers to this divine wish: YHWH himself will change the heart that cannot maintain the Horeb posture.
30: "Go and say to them, "Return to your tents."" — the dismissal of the assembly back to ordinary life: the theophany is over, the terms of the covenant declared, the mediatorial arrangement established. "Return to your tents" is the standard dismissal formula for public assembly (cf. 1 Kings 12:16: "To your tents, O Israel!"). The covenant people return to their normal domestic life carrying the covenant's claims as the structure of that ordinary life.
31: "But you, stand here by me, and I will tell you the whole commandment and the statutes and the rules that you shall teach them" — Moses's distinctive calling: to stand before YHWH and receive the full Torah that he will then teach the community. The verb teach (limmed, H3925) is Deuteronomy's characteristic educational verb — it appears more in Deuteronomy than all other books of the OT combined. Moses is not just a lawgiver but a teacher; Deuteronomy is a teaching document, not merely a legal code.
32: "You shall be careful therefore to do as the LORD your God has commanded you. You shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left" — the covenant faithfulness formula: the right-left non-turning image appears throughout Deuteronomy and Joshua (Deuteronomy 17:11,20; Deuteronomy 28:14; Joshua 1:7; Deuteronomy 23:6). The image of staying on the path without deviation is both physical (no wandering) and moral (no disobedience). In suzerainty treaties, precise compliance with the stipulations was required — partial obedience was still a treaty violation.
33: "You shall walk in all the way that the LORD your God has commanded you, that you may live, and that it may go well with you, and that you may live long in the land that you shall possess" — the covenant life-formula: walk (halach) + all the way + that you may live (lemaan tichyun) + that it may go well (vetov lachem) + that you may live long (vehaarachtem yamim). This three-part blessing formula is Deuteronomy's signature motivational structure, repeated throughout the book. The walking/way (derech) metaphor for covenant life becomes the primary metaphor of the wisdom tradition (Proverbs 1-9) and then of the early Christian movement (hodos, Acts 9:2; Deuteronomy 18:25-26; Deuteronomy 19:9,23).
